coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of sub-micronic and nanometric PMMA particles via emulsion polymerization assisted by ultrasound: Process flow sheet and characterization
coredata.dc:creator: Baissac, L.
Buron, C.C.
Hallez, L.
Berçot, P.
Hihn, J.-Y.
Chantegrel, L.
Gosse, G.
coredata.dc:description: PMMA particle synthesis was performed from MMA (methyl methacrylate) and water mixtures, exposed to different ultrasonic systems and frequencies. The sonication sequence was 20kHz→580kHz→858kHz→1138kHz, and the solution was sampled after each irradiation step for polymerization. Effects of sonication parameters (time, power), polymerization method (thermo-initiated or photo-initiated), use of small amounts of surfactant (Triton X-100™ or Tween® 20) and initial MMA quantity were investigated on particle size and synthesis yields. Particle size and size distribution were measured by DLS (Dynamic Light Scattering), and confirmed via SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy) images. Synthesis yield was calculated using the dry weight method. Particle composition was estimated using FTIR (Fourier Transform Infra-Red) spectroscopy. PMMA (polymethylmethacrylate) monodispersed particles were successfully synthesized, with a possibility of control in the 78–310nm size range. These sized-controlled particles were synthesized with a 7.5–85% synthesis yield (corresponding to 7.5–40g/L particle solid content), depending on operational parameters. Furthermore, a trade-off between particle size and synthesis yield can be proposed: 20kHz→10min waiting time→580kHz→858kHz leading to 90nm particles diameter with 72% yield in less than 40min for the whole sequence. Thus, the synthesis under ultrasound can be found easy to implement and time efficient, ensuring the success of the scale-up approach and opening up industrial applications for this type of polymeric particles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oil-water emulsification
Tandem acoustic wave
PMMA nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Process organization: Determination of the optimal operating sequence
Effect of sonication parameters on PMMA particles synthesis
Optimization of PMMA particle size and yield synthesis

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sonoreactors and sealing flange design. (a) Sonoreactors full view (b) LF sonotrode sealing flange design (c) HF transducer.
Kinetic stability of 10% v/v MMA mixture sonicated at 20kHz at 144,1W for 10min.
Influence of waiting time on the emulsification process between two first sonications and before polymerization on particle size and yields.

Particles size (a) and synthesis yields (b) evolution over tandem sonication process for SFEP thermos-initiation experiments. Influence of sonication parameters: Sequence 1 ●, Sequence 2 ■, Sequence 3 ♦, Sequence 4 ▴.
SEM image of PMMA particles prepared from 10% w/w MMA mixture with sequence 1, 580kHz sampling and polymerized with thermo-initiated polymerization method.
IR spectra of MMA and typical spectra of PMMA particles. Exposed PMMA particles spectra is from particles synthetized through sequence 4 with full sonication processing.
Particles size (a) and synthesis yields (b) over emulsification process for SFEP photo-initiation experiments for two sonication parameters: ● Sequence 1, ■ Sequence 3.
SEM image of PMMA particles prepared from 10% w/w MMA 0,15% w/w Triton™ X-100 mixture with sequence 1, 20kHz sampling and polymerized with thermos-initiated polymerization method.

Particles size (a) and synthesis yields (b) over tandem sonication process for 2,5w/w MMA%. Influence of polymerization method and use of small amount of surfactant. SFEP TI polymerization ●, SFEP PI polymerization ■, Twin 20 TI polymerization ♦, Tw20 PI polymerization (minority particles occurrence only are indicated) ▴.
Acoustic transmitted power for each available frequency for 20, 50 and 80% electric gain.
Particle size, particle population occurrence and synthesis yield obtained with sequence 1, for Triton™ X-100 or Tween® 20 addition and thermo-initiated and photo-initiated polymerization methods.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of sub-micronic and nanometric PMMA particles via emulsion polymerization assisted by ultrasound: Process flow sheet and characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UTINAM UMR 6213 CNRS Univ. Bourgogne Franche-Comté, 16 route de Gray, F-25030 Besançon, France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: UTINAM UMR 6213 CNRS Univ. Bourgogne Franche-Comté

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ELECTROPOLI GROUP, 6 bis rue Maryse Bastié, F-69500 Bron, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: PMMA particle synthesis was performed from MMA (methyl methacrylate) and water mixtures, exposed to different ultrasonic systems and frequencies. The sonication sequence was 20kHz→580kHz→858kHz→1138kHz, and the solution was sampled after each irradiation step for polymerization. Effects of sonication parameters (time, power), polymerization method (thermo-initiated or photo-initiated), use of small amounts of surfactant (Triton X-100™ or Tween® 20) and initial MMA quantity were investigated on particle size and synthesis yields. Particle size and size distribution were measured by DLS (Dynamic Light Scattering), and confirmed via SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy) images. Synthesis yield was calculated using the dry weight method. Particle composition was estimated using FTIR (Fourier Transform Infra-Red) spectroscopy. PMMA (polymethylmethacrylate) monodispersed particles were successfully synthesized, with a possibility of control in the 78–310nm size range. These sized-controlled particles were synthesized with a 7.5–85% synthesis yield (corresponding to 7.5–40g/L particle solid content), depending on operational parameters. Furthermore, a trade-off between particle size and synthesis yield can be proposed: 20kHz→10min waiting time→580kHz→858kHz leading to 90nm particles diameter with 72% yield in less than 40min for the whole sequence. Thus, the synthesis under ultrasound can be found easy to implement and time efficient, ensuring the success of the scale-up approach and opening up industrial applications for this type of polymeric particles.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nano-scaled materials have become a key factor in technical and economic development. Their unique properties emerge from the magnified specific area, compared to equivalent bulk material. Nevertheless, manufacturing processes are known to be expensive, with respect to the energy required to increase the free surface ratio, and to stabilize it.

Furthermore, polymeric particle synthesis is a very active research field. One of the main ways of producing monodispersed micronic, sub-micronic and nanometric particles is emulsion polymerization. This consists in preparing a monomer emulsion in aqueous continuous phase, followed by a polymerization step to allow particle growth in the water from dissolved monomer and monomer contained in droplets, using water soluble polymerization initiators [1,2]. Surfactants are added to water and monomer to improve emulsification rate and synthesis yield [3–5]. They are often introduced in large amounts, from 1% to 20% w/w [1,2] . Use of such compounds increases the cost of chemical supply, and implies additional operations such as purification steps, depending on particle applications. To overcome this problem, research has been carried out on polymeric particle synthesis by surfactant free emulsion polymerization (SFEP). This technic allows to synthesize highly pure polymeric particles suspensions with highly monodisperse particles. However, reaction times may be very important (several hours), and solvent are used to improve emulsification rate and synthesis yield. For instance, Shibuya et al. obtained suspension of polystyrene particles of µm size range within 1 and 3h for 11g/L and 68g/L, respectively, depending on mixture composition [6]. Camli et al. studied the influence to of acetone volume/total solvent volume ratio on PMMA particles size and size distribution [7,8]. They did not obtained particles before 20min polymerization even if they used 40% v/v acetone in the solvent mixture. Growth mechanism for SFEP synthesized particles is discussed by several authors. It is admitted that initiator attacks solubilized monomer molecule, which will in turn attack another one. The oligomer will grow up to precipitation due to increase of its polymer chain length. Then, recombination should end its growth. However, small precursor particles coagulate to form bigger and stable mature particles, because of colloidal instability [9,10].

An alternative synthesis processing consists in using ultrasound to perform emulsification. Compared with more classical synthesis route, ultrasound is reported as an effective way of reducing surfactant quantity for emulsification [11], polymerization time [12–14], and even reaction time for inorganic materials synthesis [15]. As a result, it is a helpful method for emulsion polymerization processing. Ultrasound even allowed complete surfactant suppression for monomer emulsion stabilization, which is highly interesting depending on the purity and the stability required for the emulsion. It had been used in this scope for electropolymerization [16–18], as well as for thermo initiated radical polymerization [12,13,19] and photo initiated radical polymerization [20].

Among all papers dealing with ultrasonic-assisted synthesis of polymeric nanoparticles, only few of them concerns PMMA based materials synthesis using thermo or photo-initiated polymerization, which allow very interesting yields [12,13,19,20].

Different authors reported that acoustically formed emulsions takes place in two steps. First, vibrational activity disrupts oil/water interface to form large primary droplets, which are disrupted by acoustic wave in the second step, to form smaller and stable particles [21–23]. They further found that those droplets owe their stability to adsorption of OH− or preferred desorption of H3O+ ions [24].
Nevertheless, ultrasound is reported to demulsify emulsions both with low frequencies [25,26] and with high frequencies [27,28], which means that optimum conditions may be required.

Finally, if ultrasound processing is efficient tool, it should be used carefully, especially for heterogeneous systems [29]. Indeed, designing ultrasonic reactors remains difficult for very large scale industrial purposes, even if literature is rich of such studies [25]. Indeed, misplaced transducers or wrong designed large scale reactor could lead to disturbance of the acoustic field emission and propagation, leading to processing disturbance.

Poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) is a material with many applications, such as for example medicine [30,31], optic [32], cosmetic [33], composite material elaboration [34,35], construction material [36], acoustic material [37,38]. Hirai et al. reported an ultrasound assisted emulsification route for producing submicronic and nanometric scale PMMA particles [19,20]. They showed that successive sonications of 1:10 v/v MMA:water mixture with frequencies increasing from 20kHz to 2.4MHz allowed formation of 200nm and 40nm methyl methacrylate (MMA) droplet sizes respectively, in absence of surfactant. However, this pioneering work concerns a given set of operating conditions, and the extension to additional sonication conditions or combination with surfactants, even in lower quantities than in usual emulsion polymerization, is of great interest. Furthermore, use of radical thermo-initiated (TI) polymerization was reported to enhance coalescence of droplets, leading to particles larger than initial emulsion size distribution [19]. On the contrary, radical UV photo-initiated (PI) polymerization has been reported as an efficient method for eliminating this effect [20].

The present work deals with a scale-up approach to ultrasound assisted emulsion polymerization of MMA to produce controlled size and monodispersed PMMA particles. After sonoreactor characterization, the stability of emulsions obtained at low frequency was studied, since this is a critical criterion for the following steps in the synthesis process. The effect of the successive sonications is discussed in solvent-free conditions, with two different polymerization methods. Finally, the use of small amounts of surfactants was studied as well as the influence of initial quantities of MMA. Characterizations consist of size distribution measurements by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and yield measurements through dry weight measurement. Finally, particle images were taken by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM).

MMA (≥99% purity), 2,2′-Azobis(2-methyl-propionamidine) dihydrochloride (AAPH), Tween® 20 (Tw20) were supplied by Sigma Aldrich, and ammonium persulfate (APS) (≥98+% purity) and Triton™ X-100 (TX-100) were purchased from Acros Organics. All chemicals were used without further purification. The water used for synthesis in this work is deionized water.

Low frequency (LF) ultrasound is supplied by a LabPulse 750 ultrasound generator and a 20kHz 25mm tip diameter titanium horn (SinapTec – Lezennes, France). It is monitored by NextGen software. High frequency (HF) ultrasound is supplied by a MultiFrequency Generator and a MFLG Transducer E/805/T/M, (manufactured by Meinhardt Ultrasonics – Leipzig, Germany). HF ultrasound frequencies are 580, 858 and 1138kHz. Ultrasonic cells consist of a jacketed bottomless glass reactor and are thermo-regulated, so that the temperature of the medium do not exceed 35°C. Transducers have been fixed to the bottom of the sonoreactors. To assemble the LF sonotrode to the bottomless reactor, we designed a 316L stainless steel sealing flange. An extra collar has been machined to the sonotrode, precisely on a vibration node so that the acoustic wave is less disturbed. An internally designed 316L stainless steel sealing flange has been fixed to this collar, allowing maximum power transmission and avoiding temperature rise. Fig. 1 shows ultrasonic cells and transducer design. Acoustic transmitted power is measured for each frequency using the calorimetric method [39]: see also Table 1 .

Compounds are weighed to the desired weight ratio, and total weight is fixed at 500.0g to eliminate any scale issue. Ultrasonic irradiation is then applied to the mixture. After sonication, the solution is sampled before polymerization. The first method used is TI radical polymerization. This consists in adding APS (1g/L) to the solution after reaching a temperature of 78±2°C and allowing the MMA droplets to polymerize for 15min under low stirring. The second method used is PI radical polymerization. AAPH is added (1.2g/L), and polymerization is induced for 10min under UV irradiation using a Dymax 5000-PC UV curing system (Torrington, Connecticut, USA). Mass concentration of initiators is different, but chosen to supply approximately the same radical quantity: the corresponding available radical concentration is 4.4×10−3 mol/L in both cases. If appropriate, surfactants (TX-100 and Tw20) were added to 0.15% w/w based on the whole mixture weight. For 10% w/w MMA initial quantity, this corresponds to 1.5% w/w based on MMA quantity, and to 6% for 2.5% w/w MMA initial quantity.

Particle size distributions were measured by DLS at 25°C with a 633nm laser beam and a 90.0° detector with Zetasizer 3000 HSa from Malvern (Malvern, England). Presented size values are average values of at least 5 measurements for each sample. The relative error was estimated to be equal to +/− 5% for these results. These measurements were double-checked using a Malvern Nano ZS with a 173° back scattering detector. No difference was noticed compared with the Zetasizer 3000 HSa. Furthermore, size and size distribution of particles were measured after centrifugation and redispersion for some samples. No difference was pointed out, meaning that particles undergo centrifugation purification without irreversible coagulation. Centrifugation was processed first with 17,000 RPM for 10min using an Avanti J-E centrifuge supplied by Beckman Coulter. Supernatant was removed, centrifugation tubes were refilled using Milli Q grade water (1µS/cm and 4µg/L total organic carbon), and particles were redispersed by immersing tubes into a S30 Elmasonic 37kHz ultrasonic bath for 5min. This operation was then repeated. After this second step, conductivity of the colloidal suspension dropped from 1mS/cm (raw colloidal solution) to 32µS/cm.

The colloidal solution is sampled and weighed in an appropriate container, before being introduced into an oven heated to 75°C. After total liquid evaporation, samples are weighed to obtain PMMA% w/w in the colloidal solution and the synthesis yield calculated from the following Eq. (1):
(1)
part .
synth .
yield = 100 ×
dried
particle
weight
solution
weight
×
1
initial
MMA
% w / w
The particle size measurement relative error was estimated up to 5%. Yield measurement errors are very low (related to mass measurement accuracy) and are not shown in the graphs.

Particles are deposited on a silicon wafer from diluted colloidal solutions by drop casting, and then observed with a FEG-SEM JSM 7600F supplied by Jeol, under 3.5keV (avoiding any sputter-coating on particles).

Particles were analyzed using FTIR (Bruker Vertex 70) to check absence of core/shell structure, with PMMA as shell and MMA as core. This analysis was performed between 400 and 4000cm−1 using the attenuated total reflectance mode. It consists in checking absence of the carbon-carbon double bond for synthesized particles, and the shift in ester carbonyl peak, due to the loss of conjugated π-σ-π arrangement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process organization: Determination of the optimal operating sequence
ce:section.ce:para: This part concerns the effects of operating parameters on surfactant free emulsification followed by polymerization (sonication steps or presence or absence of waiting times between two steps), with a study of kinetic stability and the results of global sequences in terms of yield and particle sizes. In all cases, initial MMA quantity was fixed to 10% in weight of total mixture, and yield calculated as per Eq. (1).

The sonicated mixture was sampled immediately after sonication, and a given volume of MMA was found at the top of the vessel. The creaming front’s height i.e. the separation between emulsion and MMA was recorded over time in graduated test-tube, and plotted on Fig. 2 . It can be seen that emulsions are very unstable in the 250s immediately after 20kHz sonication, before reaching a plateau indicating an equilibrium state of the MMA content in the mixture. As this is an important parameter, operating sequences were conducted with or without waiting times before the following step: polymerization or subsequent high frequency sonication.

Schematic processing and results of synthesis (sizes and yields) are presented in Fig. 3 . After 10min sonication at 20kHz and 140W, emulsions were sampled after different times (0–10min–405min) before thermo-initiated polymerization or transfer into a high frequency sonoreactor. The samples were then exposed to 580kHz at 28W for 10min. The final solution was polymerized immediately after (0min) or for longer times (945–1200min). If MMA forms a demixed phase at the liquid top before sampling for polymerization or HF sonication, the solution is sampled taking care to take only stable emulsion. As a comparison, a thermo-initiated polymerization was performed from a solution saturated with MMA by diffusion for two weeks: the yield is around 13%, which is close to the maximum expected from MMA solubility (15.6g/L), and particle sizes of 159nm.

First, polymerization takes place on a solution sampled immediately after 20kHz sonication. Polymerization leads to a 30% yield with 239nm particles. However, the MMA demixed phase increases continuously during polymerization, due to instabilities immediately after sonication. Emulsion is clearly not stable in the early stages after 20kHz sonication. If the same sample is transferred to the second sonoreactor for HF sonication, the MMA demixed phase is also visible during and after HF sonication. This emulsion is then sampled for immediate polymerization or for a 20h waiting time before polymerization. In both cases, no difference is noted: yield decreases from 30% to 14% due to MMA demixing. In addition, particle size drops to 164nm. To study more stable solutions, a waiting time (WT) was applied after initial 20kHz sonication. Thus, 10min were determined from the previous kinetic stability study (Fig. 3) and applied before polymerization or HF sonication. After 20kHz irradiation and 10min delayed polymerization, we obtain lower yields (23%) and smaller particles (204nm) compared to the analogous previous synthesis. In the case of 20kHz–10min WT – 580kHz sequence, similar yields (14%) and particle sizes (172nm) were obtained, if polymerization occurs immediately after HF sonication. An additional waiting time of 18.33h after HF sonication leads to similar results (163nm). Finally, a longer waiting time of 6.75h after 20kHz sonication was tested. Corresponding polymerization led to a 14% synthesis yield with a particle size of 174nm. Once again, the same sample is transferred for HF sonication. Immediate subsequent polymerization and 15.75h delayed polymerization are also performed. For immediate polymerization, synthesis yield is 13.5% and particle size 158nm, whereas 13.2% and 159nm for 15.75h are obtained for delayed polymerization.

Two key facts can be observed. If there is no waiting time after 20kHz, very high yields are attained, far greater than the yield obtained with the reference test. However, whatever the incoming step (immediate polymerization or polymerization after HF sonication), the particles are larger and the presence of a demixing phase is always observed on the top of the solution. In the case of immediate polymerization after 20kHz MMA/water mixture sonication, MMA demixing during polymerization is due to its instability, and is visible after polymerization has stopped. Moreover, PMMA is MMA soluble. Therefore, supernatant MMA could subtract PMMA from the polymeric suspension, decreasing solid content or even favoring aggregation by enhancing colloidal instability of the suspension. While we consider that particle growth is controlled via the SFEP diffusion mechanism from the dissolved monomer [9,10], the higher concentrations should be attributed to US emulsification that allows formation of quite stable MMA droplets. These droplets allow polymerized MMA to be regenerated by diffusion of MMA contained by the droplets in the bulk solution [1,2]. Thus, the most appropriate sequence can be chosen depending on process priority (yield or particle size).

To investigate the influence of additional sonication steps at higher frequencies, a 10min waiting time after 20kHz sonication followed by 580kHz was selected as it reduced MMA demixing, thus allowing more reproducible synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of sonication parameters on PMMA particles synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the previous experiments, the decision was taken to observe a waiting time after the low frequency sonication step, in order to reduce problems linked to emulsion/water separation, but to continue almost immediately with the other steps at higher frequencies. It is then possible to evaluate the complete sequences (up to 1138kHz) with various sonication times (5min or 10min) and powers (high or low, with a value depending on frequency).

10% w/w MMA mixtures were then exposed in turn to 4 different irradiations combining different times and powers for the following sonication frequencies succession: 20kHz–580kHz–858kHz and 1138kHz. Parameters can be found in Table 2 . To monitor the effect of each parameter, a sample is drawn to be polymerized using the thermo-initiated method after each sonication step (i.e. after each frequency), giving particle size and process yield as results.

All results are summarized in Fig.4 a for particle sizes and 4b for yields, plotted as a function of the frequency of the last sonication step. Firstly, and irrespective of duration or power levels applied, particle size decreases after each sonication step, i.e. while increasing frequency, whereas synthesis yield decreases. Thus, 20kHz followed by 10min waiting time allows 175–200nm particles to be synthetized with 20–25% yields. Subsequent 580kHz sonication leads to 150–170nm particle sizes with 10–14% yields. At this point, it is important to note that 580kHz sonication induces MMA demixing, which may explain the decrease in yield. Then, with 858kHz, particle sizes drop to 145–155nm, with 8.5–11.5% yield. Surprisingly, if the sonication sequence is conducted up to 1138kHz, behavior is completely different, and, in this case, greatly depends on the exposure time of the power levels used. This was not expected from previous works concerning tandem acoustic emulsification [20], where a continuous decrease is observed. This can be explained by the presence in our case of a supernatant, as our solution was never skimmed in order to keep maximum matter in the reactor to produce a maximum of usable particles. During the sonication step, previously demixed MMA is re-solubilized into the mixture, increasing the PMMA produced (acknowledged by the yield increase), but leading to an average growth of particle size. Indeed, particle sizes are far more dispersed if the last sonication step is conducted at a higher power, which corresponds to higher yields.

To conclude, it will be impossible to increase or at least to maintain high synthesis yields while decreasing particle size simultaneously. In fact, decreasing yields observed at 580 and 858kHz may be explained by demulsification mechanisms induced by ultrasound irradiation, which may promote coalescence in certain cases [27,28]. On the contrary, particle size seems to be more drivable by process parameters. According to the SFEP mechanism, if less monomer is solubilized into the solvent, fewer primary particles are formed, thus decreasing the probability of collisions and coalescence and forming so-called “mature particles” (final particles after polymerization, with size depending on both MMA droplet size and MMA available in solution) [1,2,9,10].

SEM images were taken by drop casting on a silicon wafer, revealing round-shaped particles (an example is given after 580kHz in Fig. 5 ). Particles diameters are lower than those obtained through DLS measurement. The same effect was noted and discussed by Camli et al. on a comparable system [7]. This is accounted for by the fact that DLS measurement considers hydrodynamic diameter, whereas SEM concerns dry diameter. Kazuhiro et al. obtained such monodispersity using SFEP for polystyrene particle synthesis [6].

PMMA particles were analyzed using Fourier Transform Infra-Red spectroscopy with Attenuated Total Reflectance mode. The wavelength corresponding to the stretching carbon-carbon double bound (1633cm−1), present in the case of liquid MMA, disappeared for all particles tested. Typical IR spectra of the particles and IR spectra of MMA are presented on Fig. 6 . Furthermore, some modifications are visible, and it can be noted that the wavelength corresponding to the carbon-oxygen double bond of the ester functional group shifted from 1725cm−1 to 1720cm−1 after polymerization. Wavelength shifts of several peaks is attributed to the loss of the π-σ-π conjugation, resulting in the total disappearance of MMA. The apprehended core shell structure with PMMA as shell and MMA as core is not present, and confirms particles consisting of PMMA with absence of or very low MMA content.

In this part, we managed to synthesize polymeric particles with controlled size. It was not possible to attain significantly higher yields by varying sonication parameters or subjecting to HF sonications. However, compared to more conventional synthesis routes, this work proposes a time and energy efficient alternative [14,15].

Hirai et al. showed that it is possible to reduce PMMA particle sizes prepared by ultrasound assisted SFEP by performing photo-initiated polymerization instead of thermo-initiated polymerization. However, they did not discuss the influence of the polymerization method on synthesis yield [19,20].

In this section, mixture is exposed to sequences 1 and 3 (Table 2, 10min exposure time and higher powers or 5min exposure time and lower powers). Initial MMA w/w% was kept at 10%. Particle size and synthesis yields obtained along sonication sequences are plotted in Fig. 7 .

Photo-initiated polymerization after 20kHz sonication and 10min waiting time allows formation of 95 and 110nm diameter particles (for sequences 1 and 3, respectively) with a yield of 13.7 and 16%, respectively. Immediate subsequent 580kHz sonication leads to a drop in particle size and yield for both sequences of up to 85 and 90nm, and 8.1 and 11.7% synthesis yield, for sequences 1 and 3, respectively. In this case, 858kHz sonication does not result in any changes in particle size or synthesis yield, whereas final 1138kHz sonication increases particle size (up to 110nm for sequence 1, and 120nm for sequence 3), and synthesis yield (up to 12.2%, for both sequences).

In fact, while global trends for particle size and synthesis yield evolution throughout the sonication process (Fig. 7) are similar to those observed in the case of thermo-initiated polymerization, sizes or yields are always lower at the same conditions. This cannot be attributed only to the nature of the initiator. Indeed, Camli et al. studied SFEP PMMA synthesis by using thermo-initiated polymerization with long reaction times and using both Ammonium Persulfate (APS) and 2,2′-Azobis(2-methyl-propionamidine) dihydrochloride (AAPH). They did not find these reducing effects of particle size when using an AAPH initiator instead of an APS initiator [7,8]. On the contrary, Hirai et al. [19,20] found that PI polymerization with an AAPH initiator decreased particle size compared with thermo-initiated polymerization using an APS initiator. Therefore, it may be stated that photo-initiated polymerization may greatly affect polymerization kinetics, accounting for the decrease in yield. This could result from a lower radical species generation, even if the initiator input was calculated to supply similar quantities of radical species compared with the thermo-initiated polymerization method. Furthermore, the low temperatures used here could affect mobility and interaction of radical species. Finally, the drop in particle size was accounted for by coalescence inhibition through the reduction of Brownian motion [10].

Typical SEM images have been recorded from photo-initiated prepared particles (not shown here). Once again, particles are found to be of spherical nature with a narrow distribution in size. Diameters are found to be lower than with DLS measurement. Infra-red measurements confirm the complete MMA transformation.

Abismaïl et al., have proved that small amounts of surfactant (Tween® 60) were sufficient for emulsification of high quantities of kerosene in water using low frequency ultrasound assistance, which is always better than mechanical stirring [11]. In the present study, two surfactants were added to the mixture: Triton™ X-100 and Tween® 20, with 0.15% w/w content. Tween® 20 was chosen because of its chemical structure similar to that present in the work by Abismaïl et al., with the single difference of a shorter alkyl chain. Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Balance values of Tween® 20 and Triton™ 100 are 16.7 and 13.5, respectively. These Hydrophilic Lipophilic Balance values place it within the category of oil in water emulsifier, even if Tween® 20 could also be considered as a solubilizing agent. MMA is kept at 10% w/w, and mixture is irradiated following sequence 1 (Table 2). Both thermo-initiated and photo-initiated polymerizations were tested. All results in terms of particle size and population occurrence are shown in Table 3 .

Concerning particles obtained when Tween® 20 is present, it can be noticed that average size is nearly always larger than when surfactant is absent, or at least multimodal i.e. presence of a two-particle population. As an example, after 580kHz or 858kHz as a last sonication step with photo-initiated polymerization, two main average diameters can be observed around 24nm and 65nm. Nevertheless, for particles obtained after thermo-initiation, suspensions were found to be highly unstable and measurements may be disturbed by very large particles (greater than 1µm), meaning that the values given had to be taken with care. However, this set-up offers an advantage because Tween® 20 allows very high synthesis yields to be attained.

In the presence of Triton™ X-100, it is important to note that, irrespective of polymerization mode or frequency of the last sonication step, particle populations are clearly monodispersed, with a size distribution narrowed around an average value decreasing as frequency increases, up to 175nm (thermo-initiation) or 79nm (photo-initiation) at 858kHz. Once again, if 1138kHz is the last sonication step, size increases whatever the polymerization method. Concerning synthesis yields, they are always greater than when surfactants are absent, even if they decrease while particle size decreases. This trend follows frequency, for example from 28% at 580kHz to 18% at 858kHz, but also the polymerization mode, if the latter leads to size reduction. At 1138kHz, particle sizes are 235nm with thermo-initiation compared to 117nm after photo-initiation, but the latter induces a yield decrease from 26.4% to 15.0%. Small particles could be the result of the micellar particle growth mechanism [9,10]. This mechanism suggests that some radical monomers or oligomers are stabilized by surfactant micelle, activated oligomers and activated or non-activated monomers diffusing into micelle until mature particles are formed. Because photo-initiated polymerization takes place at ambient temperature, coalescence phenomena are inhibited compared with the thermo-initiated polymerization method. As a result, the smallest particles do not coalesce. For photo-initiated prepared samples presenting a monomodal particle size population, it can be stated that for a given system, surfactant de-structuration appears for a critical frequency. This sonication affects surfactant organization and decrease micelle availability, which degrade micellar particle growth mechanism. While PMMA particles sized from 12 to 50nm syntheses are reported in the literature, these syntheses involve higher amounts of surfactant or far more complex processing tools [3–5]. Lower yields obtained using the photo-initiated polymerization method may confirm that it does not allow the same reaction progress as with thermo-initiated polymerization.

Fig. 8 is a SEM picture obtained with Triton™ X-100 using thermo-initiated polymerization followed 20kHz sonication as the frequency of the last sonication step. It confirms that well defined particles are obtained, with spherical shapes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of PMMA particle size and yield synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Another scope of study is the MMA/polymerization initiator ratio, which may introduce an additional parameter in the process operation. Indeed, it is possible that in the presence of surfactant and thus better solubilization, the initiator amount used here cannot perform complete MMA polymerization. In this case, the initiation reaction will concern only a given quantity of monomers and the propagation mode will be predominant, resulting in longer polymer chains and therefore larger particles. In this section, MMA was introduced only to 2.5% w/w instead of 10% w/w, and Tween® 20 was used to study the influence of surfactant addition to the mixture. It was chosen due to high yields obtained in the previous study. It was added up to 0.15 total% w/w, and sequence 1 was tested, followed by photo-initiation or thermo-initiation. Results are shown in Fig. 9 , with particle size and yields plotted as a function of the frequency of the last sonication step.

In absence of surfactant, influence of the frequency of the last sonication step is reduced, reducing for example the increase in final particle size at 1138kHz. On the contrary, yields are always greater, but the solid contents obtained are quite similar in both polymerization modes.

For Tween® 20 photo-initiated syntheses, a bimodal particle size distribution is observed after each sonication step. Particles population relevance are given by the DLS disposal, and is calculated from the correlation of the light beam count on the detector over analysis duration. 20kHz sonication and 10min waiting time led to particles with 88nm and 32nm for 92.5% and 7.5% particles populations relevance, respectively. Synthesis yield was 52.8% for this step. 580kHz led to 103nm and 32nm sized particles, for 95.1% and 4.9% particles populations relevance, respectively with 50.2% synthesis yield. The 858kHz sonication step reduces the particle minority population down to 3.4%. Particle sizes are 117nm and 32nm, and synthesis yield is 44.2%. Final 1138kHz sonication made it possible to synthetize 110nm and 30nm particles, with 99.7% and 0.3% signal occurrence, respectively. Synthesis yield is increased up to 63.4%. Even if signal occurrence had to be taken carefully, as it reports an intensity ratio, the final value obtained with 1138kHz as the last applied frequency is of great interest. Fig. 9 shows that two populations remain present if Tween® 20 is added and if photo-initiation is applied. Indeed, the small MMA droplets do not coalesce to form larger ones, but their presence decreases along the sonication process.

If thermo-initiation is used as the polymerization mode in presence of Tween® 20, 126nm sized particles for 85.4% synthesis yields are obtained after 20kHz sonication. The subsequent sonication step at 580kHz obtains a particle size of 121nm with 85.2% yield. The 858kHz sonication step decreases particle size and synthesis yield down to 93nm and 71.9%, respectively. The last sonication step increases particle size and synthesis yield compared with the former sonication step up to 180nm and 83.2%, respectively. It is interesting to note that the particle sizes obtained for the first 3 sonication steps are the lowest obtained using thermo-initiated polymerization. At 858kHz, particle size (93nm) is close to that obtained by photo-initiation, while on the contrary, the last sonication step at 1138kHz increases particle size up to 180nm. With thermo-initiation for low MMA contents and in presence of Tween® 20, yields obtained are the highest observed in this work and corresponds to the higher solid content for particles of less than 200nm diameter with an acceptable size distribution.

The SEM image (not shown here) shows samples collected after 20kHz step for a synthesis route using Tween® 20 and thermo-initiation, and confirms spherical shaped particles with monodispersed diameters. This observation confirms the proper functioning of the process, which provides the best shape in the view of an incorporation into a metallic coating.

PMMA particles were synthetized from MMA (methyl methacrylate) and water mixtures, exposed to different ultrasonic systems and successive sonication steps as follows: 20kHz→580kHz→858kHz→1138kHz. Various operating parameters were checked and optimized. First, the optimal succession of operations was chosen into multiple possible routes, to avoid instability of the solution due to MMA overload inducing coalescence and skimming. A waiting time of 10min was then defined after the 20kHz initial step, before any other ultrasound exposure or polymerization. The latter was conducted using thermo-initiated or photo-initiated methods throughout the study. Surprisingly, a threshold appears after 858kHz for particle size decrease, certainly due to the high MMA contents used here, required if operating in the final goal of large particle volume production. This can be partially balanced by the introduction of small amounts of surfactants, resulting in valuable solid contents of particles, with narrow diameter distribution and interesting yields. More generally, for a mixture of 2.5% w/w MMA with 0.15% w/w Tween® 20, a sequence consisting of 10min 20kHz sonication, followed by 10min waiting time and successive 10min sonication at 580kHz and 858kHz before final thermo-initiated polymerization provides a production of 93nm particle size, with narrow distribution, and with a 71.9% yield. More generally, by adjusting various ultrasonic parameters, modifying the surfactant nature or switching between thermo-initiated or photo-initiated polymerization mode, it will be possible to tune particle size between 78 and 310nm. These sized-controlled particles were synthetized with a 7.5–85% synthesis yield (corresponding to 7.5–40g/L particle solid content), depending on operational parameters. Furthermore, synthesis under ultrasound is easy to implement and time efficient, ensuring the success of the flow sheet optimization and opening up industrial applications for this kind of polymeric particles.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully thank Nicolas Rouge (University of Franche-Comté) and Frederic Herbst (University of Bourgogne) for performing SEM images. The authors thank ANRT (Association Nationale de la Recherche et de la Technologie – France) for its financial support.
